speaker: Sam Schrager
words: This conversation with Mi Lew and Marie Lee Lew took place at their home in Moscow on October 7th, 1976. The interviewer was Sam Schrager.

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: I started grade school in Spokane. Then we moved over here in 1926. I started grade school in 1920 and finished in 1926. Then I started here in 1926 and finished in 1929 at Moscow High School.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What were you interested in studying?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: In home economics. That's when I graduated the department of home economics. I've always wanted to teach home economics when I was a girl. In my young days, I thought I would go into that and teach that when I finished school. Beginning in high school, I was interested straightly going into that. That lady, there was that woman who was in that department for a long time. Miss Jensen.

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Miss Jensen. She's still in Moscow, but she's not teaching anymore.

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Those are the ones that I remember. Miss Jensen, at that time, she was teaching child development and counseling and family and dietetics and so on and so forth. Each one of the teachers had a specific...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you find you learned a great deal in that program?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Oh, yes. I learned a great deal of that even though I didn't teach after I graduated. But it still helped me in our business and in my home and in my raising of the family. It has all helped. At the time, I figured if I never go into teaching, which I was hoping to go into anyway, but if I happen to get married, I could still use that for myself.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What happened so that you didn't go into teaching?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: I was married two years before I graduated. I was married in 1931 when my future husband came back. He graduated in 1929 from Washington State. He went back to China and looked over situations there and the jobs and so on and so forth. As far as he could see, there wasn't too much of a future at that time. So he came back here in 1931.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you know each other back then?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Yes, we knew each other when he was going to school in Pullman. He was going to school in Pullman from 1925 to 1929.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you go together at that time?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Oh, we knew each other. We saw each other. We see each other at school functions between Washington State and Idaho in football games and things. But we didn't actually go together until after he graduated from Washington State. That was in June of 1929. Then he stayed here for the summer. I think he was taking a little extra curriculum during summer school, during that summer, although he graduated. But he thought he'd like to spend the time here going to summer school and probably knowing me a little bit better maybe at the time. But he has decided he would go back to China in the fall of 1929 to look for a job and see what the situation was over there at that time. But when he went back from 1929 to the fall of 1929 until the spring of 1931, he saw that there wasn't too much future in his engineering career there at the time. He could almost foresee the Chinese war coming on. Things were very disturbed there. The situation was bad as far as government jobs for civil service jobs. His fear would be building roads and buildings, and he doesn't see very much future in that with things coming on.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would he have had trouble getting a job as an engineer here because he's Chinese?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: He would have a harder job to get a job here because he is a Chinese than the American people, the American boy graduating from same class. Because he can see that during his time, like he had said before, that the small racists are discriminated against. When he went on field trips with his schoolmates, he was not allowed to sit in the same place in the movies as they were. He was not allowed to give him a haircut at certain places where his schoolmates were allowed to have haircuts. I don't think he ever tried to eat at the same places, but he didn't say very much about discrimination against him there. But he did feel very badly about that all the other schoolmates on these fields were able to buy a ticket and go to a show, and he could not sit with them. He bought the same ticket and paid the same price, but he could not sit with them. He had to be segregated in a different section of the theater. This was at college, somewhere around 28 or 29. That is not in Pullman now. I'm not saying that. But on field trips, like when he went to Spokane, especially when he went to Spokane, at that place he definitely remembered the Pantasius Theater in Spokane at that time was opening. And he was not allowed to go in.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it a section where anyone was a minority group sat?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: I guess so.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Like black people too?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Yes, black people, Indian or Negroes. Yeah, well, black people. And Chinese would have to be there too. And only whites could sit where the rest of the people sit.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about for you, if you had wanted to become a teacher, would it have been tough for you at that time?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: I think at that time it would have been a little bit tough for me too. It certainly wouldn't be as easy as it is for my daughters to be getting a teaching job now. Nowadays, they look at your record and they give you a job according to your record in school. But in those days, well, if you're not a... Well, at that time we were not American citizens because we were not allowed to become American citizens until 1943. So when I graduated in 1933, I was not an American citizen yet. But of course, I never tried to get a job as a teacher because, like I say, I went into the restaurant business in 1932. When my husband and I were married, we went into the restaurant business together. And he needed me to help him there, and so I never looked for a job. But of course, I didn't graduate until two years afterwards.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But you continued to go to school?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Yes, I continued and finished school in 1933.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What kind of arrangements did you make with your father to take over the business at that time?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Oh, uh -huh.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did he sell it to you?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Yes, we bought it from him just like anybody else would buy it, just like he would sell to anybody else. With the stipulation, of course, that we couldn't pay him for the whole thing all at once. So we had to pay him back in monthly installments. Because when we started, we didn't have any money. I was still going to school at that time, and although the tuition wasn't very high, but it still cost a little bit of money. And during that time, money was scarce. We were married in the summer of 1931, and the fall of 1931 was not too bad. But beginning March of 1932, when all the banks were closed, that was really bad then. So my second semester, well, the second semester of my third year is beginning... Well, in fact, my third year was beginning to be bad already. Business was not as good as it had been before, and things were slowing down. And there wasn't too much business at the time when we bought the restaurant over. But like everything else, there wasn't very much business any place else either. So at that time, for what money we did not have, that was the best situation we could get, was to buy it for my folks. Because they wanted to sell the place because they wanted to take the younger children back to China to give them a little bit of Chinese culture when they were still young.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did they do that?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Yes, they did that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So they went back, and did they stay or come back again?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Yes, they stayed. But in 1937, when the Japanese -Chinese War were in Shanghai and Nanking, my folks settled in Nanking after they went back there, because they see that the future was better in Nanking than down south in Canton. That is for them. So they settled up in the north, and then they stayed there until the Japanese -Chinese War started. And then they sent my two younger brothers and sisters back here to the states. They were born here in the United States, being citizens, where they were able to get passes back as soon as they could get room on the ships. But at that time, the ships were all crowded, and so they had to wait about a month or six weeks before they could... No, about a month anyway. And they had to go from Shanghai. They were hoping to get a ship from Shanghai to come over the United States, but not being able to, they went south and went down to Canton and to Hong Kong. And they were finally able to get a book passage there, because everybody who are U .S. citizens, they are coming back over here to the states then, and the ships were all crowded. But they did finally get back here, and Mai and I, the only ones being over here, that we could look after them when actually they sent my brother and sister over here. In 1937, for us to take care of them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it hard for you to keep going to school during the Depression at that time?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Well, it wasn't too hard, no. Since I have my board at the restaurant, you know, and I have my husband, I have an apartment in town. We were able to have enough tuition, you know, for me to go through school. And of course I kept working at the restaurant and helping Mai, and we didn't have too many help at that time, because, well, during the Depression you just don't have too much business. But we still had one extra cook that we hired, and one or two waitresses. We wanted to keep them, although we weren't able to pay them every month, you know. All their wages, they were willing to stay on, because there wasn't any other job, any place else for them either. So as long as they stay on with us, if an one condition gets better, and we can afford to pay them back for their back wages, we would. They know that we would, and so they stay with us.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Could you do that? As soon as that happened, finally, that you could pay them?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Oh, yes. Yes, we pay up all of what we owe, you know. Well, when you never had too much money to spend, you know, we just don't have the habit of spending any money. As a girl, well, we were not exactly very poor, but we were not rich either, you know. It's just an average family. My father had a business, you know. As a girl, my father just had a business, and I was always, as children, always have to help in the restaurant business.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So what you would spend on, what would it be, just clothing?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Well, just very little on clothing, and what has to be spent, has to, like books and things we have to have, well, we have to buy those. And then whatever there's a little bit left over, then it goes into clothing. And at that time, I know we didn't even get to go to a show very often, not even once a week at that time. Well, in the first place, you know, when there isn't too much business and not too much money, you have to work a little bit harder. You get pretty tired, you know. By the end of the working day in the restaurant, you want to go home and get some rest and sleep.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And you were going to school?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: And I was going to school, too, in the meantime.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, was there much social life when you were going to school?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Usually, the Chinese, well, at that time, the Chinese girls and boys doesn't have too much social life with the American people. But we do have what we call the international clubs, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What is it?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Cosmopolitan Club. At that time, there were quite a few Filipino boys and some Chinese boys, but mostly boys sent over here from their own country. Now, the Chinese people have some scholarships, you know, from the, I think it's from the Boxer Rebellion or back in quite a few years back, that they were still allowing some Chinese, boys to come over here to study. Although these boys wouldn't be from a poor family, they are from average, above average families that have a certain amount of money to buy their clothes, you know. So, the scholarship is only for boarding, maybe part of the room and all tuition fees, like out of state and all that. But they still, they have, you know, from their own family, they have to support them otherwise. So, well, as far as the Chinese people, as far as we're concerned, that's about the only social work we have. And we do have ministers from Moscow and Pullman that work with us, you know, in our group that are interested in us. And one of them is Reverend Drury from the Presbyterian Church. He's the one that married us in 1931 because we know them pretty well. And he was with our group in the cultural department. He was interested in the foreign students, you know, and there were quite a few of the professors, too, that are interested and they sponsor us.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What kind of activities would you have, social ones? Would they be dancers and all that?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Not too much dancing because we don't do too much dancing. But we get into a group and we would have a coffee or tea and an ice cream or cake or something. And then we would talk, you know, and we would discuss maybe mostly our own school work and our concerns with our everyday activity and with the church maybe and very little in politics because we have no vote anyway. But we do and we are interested in what's going on. At least we know what's happening anyway, you know, especially around our two, well, quite often we have the joint cosmopolitan practice between Washington State and Idaho. And we would go out on picnics and we would go out on hikes, you know, up to Moscow Mountain or someplace like that. And we would make a whole day of it either on a Sunday or on a Saturday or when the weather is good. But few girls, mostly boys. Yes. There are just a few girls. That's because there were few that had come. Yes. Most of the families would send their boys out to get educated because usually they figure that the boys are the ones that to make a living for the family and they should get educated first.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And it was mostly Filipino and Chinese and were there Japanese too?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Not too many Japanese. Mostly, well, at that time the Filipinos, as far as I know, when they come here they have a different status than that because you see they were under US control, you know, and I think they could come and go without too much red tape. We, the Chinese, and I don't know about the Japanese, I think they come a little bit easier even, but the Chinese have a really hard time getting over red tape and all that. And they can only come over as a student and they can only go as a student and they cannot work over here and as soon as they're through school they get a little bit of room and they have to go back. That's Japanese. Chinese. I don't know too much about the Japanese, but the Japanese people I know they can come over here a little bit easier than the Chinese. They have a better quarter and I heard if the Japanese government would allow them to come over they can almost get 100 % sure that they would allow them to come in over here to go to school.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: This is now or back then?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Then. Even then. So now it should be more liberal. So that means really that the Asian people were not really, couldn't, and were expecting not to go to the dances and those things that they were campus.

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Well, I don't know, I don't think there are too many of the dances where they would mix, well as far as I know I was the only Chinese girl going to the university at the time. And I, as far as I know, there were only two at the most three Chinese girls at Washington State at that time. So there aren't too many girls as far as Chinese girls are concerned. And Japanese is about the same. And I'm not saying anything very much about the Vietnamese or any because at that time I don't think we have too many of those people, you know, that race. Those races come along. It was really a strict, a strict situation. A strict situation, yes.

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: I think so as far as school is concerned.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, how did that make you feel at the time?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Did it seem that, because it seems to me, all besides being unfair, it seems, it seems kind of difficult to expect people to, to do that. Well, it didn't to me because I, well, for one thing, it makes the student, I mean, our race of students study a little bit harder because they have more time to study. They have less time to go out socially then. That's one good thing out of it. For another thing, well, I myself, it's more or less an introvert and I don't go out and, well, you know, barge myself into other things. I don't care too much for dancing because I never had it. Well, I never went out, you know. I was pretty well kept at home during my grade school and high school years. And in college, yes, I was still staying home under supervision of my father and mother. And being Chinese, you know, I strictly, I just go to school to go to school to learn. And as far as, and besides, I have to spend some time in the work too, you know, to help the folks out.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, do you think that the kids saying the Cosmopolitan Club would read that they would read that?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: I'm imagining if it was me, I imagine that I would react by thinking that I'm feeling that I was better than all those other kids anyway. And I was just wondering if that was a way that, that, that some of the kids maybe would feel, you know, that you think that they think that they are a little bit better than the American people. No, I was saying is that I was, I was thinking that that's a natural way that would be to react to think that. Because that's how, that's how I imagine I would feel. That's how I felt when I've been excluded, say from a group and that sort of thing. I feel like they think they're so, they're so much better. Yeah. Well, as far as I'm concerned, they can think that that much better. It's all right with me, you know, because it doesn't matter to me. What I want is at that time is mostly in education anyway. And the faster I get it, the better I'm off. Maybe the sooner I get out there and, and getting to a better job maybe and bring in more money for myself and for my family. So I don't miss it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How much friendship was extended, you know, to you from the, from like the other girls that were in, were in Home Act? Were they very outgoing?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Yes. And leave you alone. Oh no, that my old, well, Home Economics is most, is all girls anyway at that time. So as far as I'm a girl, they, I am just one of the girls with them, with my own Home Act girls. I don't think I was excluded from any of their things. I go to all the, whatever's going on, I go, I go to it. And I was invited to all of them. So as far as Home Economics, the department's concerned, all the teachers treat me just as well as anybody else. There is, there is no distinction. I don't feel any distinction between their attitude toward the other student and their attitude toward me. So as far as I'm concerned, I made these because I took that course, you know, in this particular field. Because I like it, that's why I take it. And, and it just happened to be one of the, one of the things that the mostly all girls. So I was very satisfied with my love, with my life in school. And I do have a few very close friends that I have made and I have continued with, you know, from the Home Economics departments and also a few that are not in the Home Economics, but the few girls that have lived in town here in Moscow, because they went to high school with me. And, and, and, well, I think all the, everybody in Moscow has treated my family and me. And, and now my husband and I, you know, after, afterwards my husband and I, just like, well, that is the people that know us. We were never discriminated against, as far as I know. And we, they were friendly to us, at least to our faces anyway. And we, like I said, we're, we're the kind of people that doesn't go out too much. We don't associate too much. We don't go for dancing, no drinking, no smoking. So you see, that excludes a lot of stuff from us, you see. We just have a few friends that we like to entertain in our own home and we go to our friend's home and we just enjoy ourselves that way. And business, business -wise, I think we have been treated very well in Moscow. As far as, well, there's always one or two exceptions, you know. They might, well, maybe they don't like our race in the first place and then so they get that against us too, you know. And so maybe they don't want to deal with us. Well, those people, well, we just don't deal with them. You know, if they don't want to deal with us, we're not going to budget and deal with them because there are other people that would deal with us that, that we could associate with in business, you know. As far as I can say, we've always, whenever we need anything, whenever we need, we need anything. We need to buy anything. We need to buy any services. We are all well treated. In medicine, you know, in doctorings, in other doctors, we're well treated. And department stores, we're well treated. And all the supermarkets, grocery stores, and the car dealers, or the agencies, the travel agencies, they're all, you know. Well, you know, I think about some of your kin and other people that you know with Chinese. There must be some cases that you know of where really people didn't get treated well and when they should have been. At least I imagine there must have been because...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Because of the race?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Yeah. Well, particularly in my family, I don't know of anybody that wasn't treated. I thought about any of my, you know, the Chinese people as a whole, we don't want to butt into other people's things if we're not wanted. Because if they don't want our business, we just don't go to their business. If they don't want us to go to their church, I wouldn't say that because all churches welcome us as far as I know of. All the churches in Moscow are members, you know, although we go to the Methodist Church. But the members of the other churches have been very friendly with us. And the historical women has been good, you know, to me and the senior citizens. We get along very well with them, you know. So as far as I know, none of my family around here, I know, I don't think we have ever been ill treated by anybody.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, do you think that it changed, say, in time period?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Because, you know, I mean, the record from the early days, like with the gold miners in the area, this is terrible. They don't talk about it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: In the enormous case, you know, how far is that from the church?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: It wasn't too long ago that the far objectives of Mary Anderson went down to Paschal and they were like... If you went down to the Russians, there's not Paschal. And Paschal, when she was supposed to give or sing at a place. I can think of a very... I imagine it has been about 30 years ago. About 30 years. The last 25 years is different. Before that, when I went to school in 25 and 26, there is a separation here quite a bit at that time. Of course, she went to school later than I did. So therefore... I went in 29. Therefore, it gradually changed a little bit then.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, how did you experience it at that time? What were the kinds of things that were where there was separation of the people?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Well, I don't know. I wasn't wondering what the school was there in Poland, you know, in the first year I went there. Well, of course, the teachers and the instructors and everybody wanted to go to church.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Who is the young people?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: So I went to the church in the way it was called. At that time, there was separation quite clear at that time. Therefore, I and my friend and another Chinese boy went to the church with the young people. The BYU, where Baptist young people were. We went there. It just happened after the meeting. It just happened that they had a little refreshment served. You know, after the young people's meeting. After the refreshment served, then they went to line. The boys on one line and the girls on one line. There we go and get our cake and a little coffee. Then we're supposed to pair off because the boys and girls pair off. I know the Chinese boy and I were both there. Of course, we didn't ask her. At that time, we just come that way. I noticed distinctly that the two girls didn't want to feel their shame, probably, that they had not caught us. The two girls, since at the church they tried to wake up, they didn't. They just faced each other. The two girls were inside, the two boys. I sat on the one outside, the one girl, and the other boy sat on the outside. The two girls were just eating for themselves. We just sat out there, but we didn't say anything. We were called. Of course, that made us feel, you know, it didn't right. When you ask us to go to the church, the church of where you are, all men are created equal, and the hair of your fields towards that way, so distinctly that way. So that time, from then on, more or less, we just said, don't go so much. That's all. We just asked us to make some goals. We'll go to church and that ends it. We don't have those that deal with anybody because we just sat in church. That's all. We didn't go home. I know distinctly that was really, you know, it hurts. Yeah, it hurts, you know, especially when you tell somebody, Christian, you love, you call it, then you turn around and do that, you know, in the church of God, you know, in the house of God. That creates a kind of a feeling in what you are. In other words, it's kind of a hypocrite, a wave of doing things. That's why there's lots of Chinese. They get the missionaries in China that way. The missionaries in China, the big companies standing over there to check up on the Chinese, what they want. They don't want the time to come for them to defend themselves. They run out first. They defend themselves. In case of revolution or anything like that, you know, you're a great poet. You know, you can't even lie to her for it. Here you are, you run first. When it comes to the way it's called, you're a leader, and the leader runs first. You know, that way. Mrs. Lewis said that when you were in Spokane, that you had to sit there. Yeah, same thing. I went to a movie with a basketball game with my friend. Just field trip. We went to a basketball game. And after the basketball game, we went to the Pantages Theatre. Then a whole group of us, you know, just a whole group of boys, because we all go into the white cops. And then they divided me up. All everybody else, the other boys go and sit downstairs. They push me upstairs. I had the WSC hat on, all the rest of them, just the same thing. And we all went together.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Who did that? The man that runs the stage? Pantages himself, the whole man, what do you call it? Are you sure it was Pantages in Seattle?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Yeah, yeah. All that time, that way. All that time. The barber shop, you used to be that way, too.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That mean you couldn't go in a bar and try to get a haircut?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Only the crummy ones.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: A certain, what do you call the first class barber shop?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: The high class barber shop. Yeah, but when you go to the whole of the cornies, that's the way you call it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was that true in Poland and Moscow too?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: No, Poland and Moscow are not that way. No, I could say that. They are not that way. But Walla Walla was that way.

speaker: Marie Lee Lew
words: Walla Walla was? Did they ever tell you, when you were at college, did they let you know that you shouldn't go to the social work?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: No, no, no, no.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did they tell you?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: No, no, no, they don't say anything anyway.

speaker: Marie Lee Lew
words: As far as I can tell, the Chinese people are not very, what do you call it?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: They are not that social. Yeah, you know, you have to kind of drag them if you want to go there first, especially the first time. You have to kind of drag them the first time because they hate to go there and feel like I went to that church that time. You know, they asked me a minute time to go there. Then I finally went. They figured, I don't know, when you got to that point, then I didn't feel good. And then, you know, then I don't go anymore. You know, that's how it is. You know, Chinese people are very, very wake up. They brainwash somebody's feelings or something. They didn't like to color people. Now they color people. Now they, oh boy, they fight for their rights. In a way, I don't blame them for fighting for their rights. But nevertheless, sometimes they go the other extreme too. Yeah, you go the other extreme. Well, so what they did in a way, it sounds like, was they lumped all people that weren't white together. Yeah, that's right. That's right. That's right.

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: That's right. They call it color for the people. Straighten it, not black, but all the A with the group themselves. And there's a few teachers that are very much interested in foreign students. They're very few of those. And then there's one of the more American white people that, you know, are students that likes the Chinese. Yeah, yeah. I like that. They would go in there too, but not too many. Yeah, there's a few. See, part and part, that doesn't mean all color, you know. I mean, it means everybody. Yeah. So there's usually one or two students that probably already befriended some of the other, the older students, you know, and they like the Chinese. And so they want to join that group too, you see.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were there any blacks in that group?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: At that time, there weren't many blacks at all. No, I don't know any school. Well, most of these people going to be going back to the Asian after school. They weren't permanent in their area. Yeah, most of them, we are. Most of them is white. At that time, I think they only had one black white student there when I was there. Of course, at that time, permanent only had 3500 people. At that time, about 32, 3500 people. They were very many. And they're far exactly turned out. I think they only had 11 Chinese people there. And just one more. There was quite a few Filipino boys there, the Filipino boys, because they came in through, what do you call it, the Filipino, what do you call it, they were still there. I imagine 15 or 20 of those. But the outside that there isn't very many, the Indians are practically no Indians over there at that time.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you develop any close friendships, many close friendships with the white Americans when you were at home?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Oh, I had some friends, yeah. But I had the friends that went to school with me in high school. See, a lot of my friends went to high school, and they went to graduate from Walla Walla, so they went up to Pullman.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh, you went the same time?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Yeah, we were all graduating in class. Some of them moved up there. Yeah, I had some friends that I already called. Of course, when they go to university, then... But when you go to university, you see. Then they're, what do you call it, the divergent. You take your course and you take their course. Then therefore, that's the age when the boys date the girls over there. But we don't have any, because we haven't got any Asiatic girls there at all. We just don't do anything. That's all. We did work, that's all. That's why most of the students, Asiatic students, or foreign students, are better. They're a better student, because they have no place to go. They're better as a student. They're better as a student, because that's all they've got to do, is just to study. Although some are hard for them, because they have to learn the language, learn the ways, and learn the way they call it. Well, for our Asiatic students, I had a kind of hard time, too, because there are lots of things that I don't know what they're talking about. There are many things, and American people have in their homes, that they have, that we don't have in our homes. So what they're talking about sometimes, I don't know what they're talking about. Then you have to look, and then all that. There is a hindrance in that way, in that respect. In fact, when Mi came over here as a child, his mother didn't come over here, though. His mother is one of those Chinese who, his father didn't bring her over here, so his father just brought him over here. When he was six years old, six, seven years old. At that time, they couldn't bring him over anyway. No, they don't allow him when they come over. Let's see, 1921 or 1922 before the left. At the time when we came over, that's about one of the first years. Pretty first, when they allowed businessmen, wives to come. If they are citizens over here, they cannot bring their wives over here. At that time, that's the law. I don't know why either.

speaker: Marie Lee Lew
words: Now, if you're a citizen, no matter what your wife is, the wife gets to come over here, see? But at that time, as far as I know, if you're a citizen, you cannot bring your wife, isn't it Mike?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: I think that's a law. But he couldn't become a citizen. He had to be born here. He had to be born here. He cannot become a citizen. Not in 1943. Nobody can become a citizen.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When you were growing up in Walla Walla, were there ever any times when you had to stand up for your rights, or when you fought over it?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Oh, yes, there are certain times, especially when I was growing up there at that time, but there's a bunch of Russians in Walla Walla. There's what's called Russian kids. They got the Italians. They're quite a lot of Italian farmers. They got a Russian town. They call it a Russian town. And they got the Italians what they call too, the Italian farm. The Italian people, they sometimes get kids out. They don't know any better. They make fun of the Chinese people. And the Russian people, they think they're the Russian kids. They think they're the sister of white. They pick on the Chinese people. But the one example of the Chinese people is that they only eat a terem, but the Chinese people, they stick together. When you get the only tesvom, they can get the Russian to lick anybody. They come along. The kids can do that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You mean you kids travel in a gang pretty much?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: No, if they start picking, then even though you don't need to start in a gang, it's in the playground, especially in the playground. That's where they start the white cause in the playground. They make fun of you. Because the Chinese people, the Chinese people, they stand around the way they call it. Because they don't play here, and they don't play football, they don't play anything like that. Anyway, most of those are when they come over, about 10, 11 years old, and they come over, so they stand around and they wait until the bell rings, and they go back there. So they do, because the Russian kids, they pick on them and try to do something, but the Chinese people, when one of them picks the other one, then the other one will jump in on them. They help them out. So after they help them out, the one thing about the teachers, they're always on the Chinese side. They're always on the Chinese boy side. The Chinese people are not the one that's stopped. You put your nose in the other side. Yeah, yeah, yeah. They're always on the Chinese boy side.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How would they start fighting with you? Would they call the kids' names?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: First thing, they call the kids' names, and then they throw things at you. They throw little rocks, and the way you call it that kid. They pick on you, and if they want to do it, throw rocks at you. Then they throw back, and then they fight with you. You can be a big bully, like a big bull come along, and then try to wait for it. That's how they do it. The Russian, they pick on the little kids. They're the smaller ones. But the big ones are there. See, the way they call them, the big ones will help the little ones out.

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Now, what did you get the Italians coming in?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: The Italians, the Italian farms.

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: The Italians get after the Chinese?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Oh, yeah, they do. They call us chinks, and they call us all the other names. They call them names. Of course, the Chinese won't... It doesn't take long for them. We learn to call them, they go too. So they get irritates on their mind.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about in high school? What about up in high school? Were there kids that you think didn't have anything to do with you or didn't want to have anything to do with you because they were prejudiced? Were their parents prejudiced in high school?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: No, high school is a little bit better. High school, the high school, they get into a group. Usually, the wealthier families, their family, their kids, they get into a little class amongst themselves. They do anything, they run the thing, and they do anything. Then the people are poor. You don't change who's the only one who's weak. Then you don't get up there with them. So you go down to lower class of people, and then your class is thinking in that way. And of course, the upper class doesn't have nothing. The upper class people got nothing to do with the lower class, especially the Chinese people. We just Chinese. I was the first Chinese. I was the only Chinese boy. But at that time, I took ROTC. Of course, at that time, I took ROTC. It was very good that I took ROTC because I had ROTC clothes to wear. I got the clothes just the same as they have. Everybody's the same, uniform, in other words.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So you wore uniform to school?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Oh, yeah. You can wear uniform to school. They have four years of ROTC, one -on -one.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You wore every day?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Yeah, you wore it every day. Nobody said anything. It's real good that way. I wore it every day.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You're the only Chinese boy in the high school? What happened to your friends? They didn't go out to the high school?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Most of them finished eighth grade, they quit. They go to work. Their parents don't believe in that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You went? Well, I said, what just happened to my parents?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: They wouldn't believe in it. That's all my parents. He believed in education. His father believed in education. Yeah, my father believed in education. That's how it comes. As far as I can tell, my father was the only Chinese man that educated her daughter. My sister was a doctor, you know, MD. She's the only one who believes in it. Everybody says she was crazy to do that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: She's an MD? Is she a doctor?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: No, no, she was in China. But she was an MD, you know. But everybody says she's crazy. That he's crazy to do that. Oh, yes. Because, you know, after all, the little girls go to the American people or someone else. In other words, you're educating somebody else's wives. Well, that's the way they are. A lot of American people feel the same way. They're like the American people feel the same way. The husband's the same way.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did your father feel about it then? What was his answer to that?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Well, he just believes in education for everybody. That's all. He just believes in education for everybody. He thinks it would be better off if everybody had an education. And that's the way my father feels, too. Maybe he had a little more respect for his daughter than some of the girls. There are some, too. But another example here is that we had a Chinese girl that graduated from Fulton College. She's a 5 -beta candidate. She was a real smart girl. She was the only Chinese girl that graduated from Fulton College at that time.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did she do?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: She couldn't get a job. She was behind you, though. No, she's above me. She's an old man. She couldn't get a job. She ever got a cell noodle for her brother. And then she died here for about a few years back. Just two years ago. Yeah, yeah. 80, 80, 90 -year -old. She had Baker. She's a very brilliant girl.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Is that because she was Chinese?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Yeah, she was a woman. Because of her both. More likely both. Chinese in the first place, a woman in the second place. Yeah, that's right. Otherwise she's a brilliant girl. She's a that kind of girl. She can go teach her or something. Right now she can teach any of our places. She didn't. She couldn't get a job. The world is different changing now, I agree with you. You figured that...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would you have been able to get an engineering job someplace if you'd wanted to, or because you were Chinese, you couldn't then?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Well, I couldn't attend anyway. Because at that time, engineers only $75 a month, even higher at that time.

speaker: Marie Lee Lew
words: Even over here?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: The first place had to become a citizen.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Before you got to be a citizen, to be an engineer?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Yeah, and to work in the United States for a project you do. Or to work for the government. Or work for anybody. Unless you got enough money to be a contractor yourself. That's my type of money.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You mean citizenship was a requirement to be hired by a company?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Even go to transit the road, or even such simple things as to hold a line, or a rod or something. You have to become a citizen. For any project. Even to go get a gun to shoot a squirrel, you have to become a citizen before you have a gun. You know, you have to be a citizen before you have a gun.

speaker: Marie Lee Lew
words: Today? Today?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Yeah, you shoot a squirrel. You get to be 22 or something like that. Maybe be a gun, I'm not going to get along all right.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You couldn't have been hired by the college, or by a business, or anything, to be an engineer?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: No.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So is that the reason why you were looking at going back to China?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Yeah, at that time I was looking to go back to China and I said because of that, I couldn't go back and get a job there. But the things are changing back there that don't look good. I can see war coming. That's what you see. Yeah, I can see war coming. I just feel it in the air. The war coming and every so often they put propaganda in it. And they came to the way, you guys do this. And every so often they put propaganda in the company and they say you do that. It wasn't too long. It wasn't too long until I came back 31, 32, the war started in Japan with the Manchuria. You came back in 31 and 32 was the one they started. Yeah, in Manchuria.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When you went over there, did you feel in yourself more like an American than you did like you belonged in China?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: I mean, you didn't know China very good at the time. No, you don't know China, but nevertheless, you do feel pretty good because you're amongst your own people. Your own people respect you. See, you're not the, they say Mr. So and So, they always invite you. Anything special comes on, they invite you. Over here, you don't, no matter what, you're not one of them. So, thank you. So, they accepted you and they didn't feel like you were foreigner. No, no, no, no, no. When you're over there, in other words, you're more like a aristocrat. You're like a doctor. He's a United States graduate, you see. So, he's better than a lot of the other people, you see. The average people, even. Over there, only the very wealthy can go to college and go to college. So, really, that meant that you kind of moved up in the social class. There are no doubt about that. There are no doubt about that. More wealthy people would invite him to their weddings. The cultured people would do it. It's the same thing here. You're, well, you're the only the wealthy people, you know, to do the same thing with you. You know, certain group would do it, certain group would not invite you, you see. Yeah, you feel better because you're, they respect you. Wherever you go, they respect you. Well, you're one of them. Yeah, you're one of them. Where you're here, you come in. I don't know a class of people. Yeah, when you start releasing your, when they see our color.

speaker: Marie Lee Lew
words: You see?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: You go into even, even, even. Well, colored people, you know, that's why colored people feel that way.

speaker: Marie Lee Lew
words: When we register at the Hilton or, or at any of the better class hotels, you know, they look at you, you know, wonder how, how could they afford a place like this? What is this family, you know, the hotel that, let's see, children going free?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: No, holiday.

speaker: Marie Lee Lew
words: And the holiday in?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Well, we were there in San Francisco and we stopped and we stayed at the holiday in, like $49, $50 room, you know, for just two people. And I see legal people there, you know, China, well, of course, this one is styling right in Chinatown, see. That's where we want to stop because we don't want to have to take a bus or take a taxi every time we go to China. So we just stop right there, you see. And we see, of course, most of them in Chinese anyway.

speaker: Marie Lee Lew
words: And, well, at least 50 % and 50 % American people and very few percentage, a Negro, you know, well, you just wonder how could those people, Negro people can afford or Chinese people can afford?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Well, of course, only the wealthier people can travel, you know, and can afford a $50 a day hotel, you know. You can't do that 350, two days a year and neither can we. But when we go, because of the convenience and, well, that's besides, we got on a deal anyway, you know, a package deal, which didn't amount very much to us, you know, because we buy the whole package.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, do you think that most of the people that were over here would have wanted to go back home if they could? I mean, would have preferred to be back there because they would have felt more at home there if they could?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Yes, in the old days. In the old days they would because they do feel better. They do. They are among their own people and they can go just as far as anybody. But over here, you don't go to the top 100%. You know, as far as that can turn you. Maybe you get 75%. As far as that can turn, even not too long ago, you try to get into the large, you try to go into the end of the large, they won't let you in. Our friends invite us to go in there, you know, we check. No, no, no, no way. We told our friends, they said, well, we cannot join your club, you know. The moose is nothing, you know, as far as the club's concerned, maybe the elves are there. Well, as far as I'm concerned, you know, we don't join clubs anyway because, you know, it's a social function club and you go, well, if you don't drink and you don't smoke, you're kind of out of it anyway. We don't play golf, we don't bowl, you know. So, we're not in that, you know, with that class of people. Well, I'd like to do a restaurant business though. That pretty much protected you from that kind of stuff. I mean, then you didn't have to depend on anybody else for a job. Yeah, that's right. That's the reason why we went in the restaurant business at that time. That's the reason why there's so many foreign people go into the restaurant business, have a little business of your own. Yeah, and then you got a little, you know, then you work your family together, see, the way you call it. That's the only, I think, well, that's probably the only kind of business. Well, and their laundry and their grocery store. Of course, in the bigger towns, they have other, you know, into the department stores and then, you know, the Japanese go into a... The Japanese have been offered here, you know. I know the Japanese have been offered here. They have a... The government agreement that the Japanese can come in. You can not get away from it. As long as the Japanese authorities let them out of Japan, they can come in. Then if you do something, call it, because the Japanese ambassador will jump right on your tailbone, see whoever makes a rebound. Yeah. The Chinese people are not that way. They're weak. Well, I think in the first place, we're not the kind that would jump on anybody's tailbone anyway, you know, unless we're pushed so hard that we have to fight back, you know. We're more of a mild temper people anyway, and we just kind of let things take its course, you know, in a way. We try to do the best we can under the circumstances, but we don't try to fight too much with the circumstances. You're friends like the ones that... You're friends that weren't Baker, for instance. Baker is a town that had a pretty bad reputation in the early days and some of the stuff that, you know, happened with Chinese miners in the country around there. I know it was really rough.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I'm just wondering, do you think they were as well accepted in a place like Baker?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Oh, yes. Afterward. Afterward. As early as... This is in the late years, you see. This is in our time. I would say she's about eight years older than my age, you see. So five to eight years. So you see, she just passed away a couple of years ago. And she never did very much with her education, that is. I mean, she didn't get a chance to do it. She had to run a restaurant, too. She was selling noodles at the back of the factory. She was running a sales lady for selling noodles at the Chinese places all through the Northwest.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did she live in Baker at that time?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: No, she was afterward. Yeah, she was a Walla Walla. She was a really important girl. She was important. I thought she was a Walla Walla girl. No, she's important. She graduated from Whitman College. I know, but I thought it was because she lived there. Because at that time, Whitman College would be considered one of the better schools. In fact, there are still these. And she graduated from there. Well, of course, you know, I mean, a few things didn't come just quite right with her either, you know. Over circumstances, maybe she didn't take the opportunity when the opportunity was there and it was too late or whatever, you know. I think partly because she was so well educated, so much about the other people, the average Chinese people are afraid of her. Because she's so much more free. Yeah, we're looking for a wife.

speaker: Marie Lee Lew
words: Who could marry her if they're married?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Yeah, afraid of her. Yes, afraid to court her.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: She's too smart?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: She is. She's too smart. The first Chinese girl that graduated is Whitman. And I think Bob, the first one, graduated from here as far as I know. There might have been some before, but as far as I know, I think I was the first one here. But that didn't scare you off because she was... But earlier than that, there weren't too many Chinese there, so she didn't marry. No. No. She never married and she just went to work for her well. They say you graduated in 1929, probably she graduated in 1920. I would say 20 or 21, 22. Well, and another thing too, maybe she was not...

speaker: Marie Lee Lew
words: You say she was born in Portland?

speaker: Marie Lee Lew
words: Yeah, she was born in Portland. Well, then she's a citizen. See, like Marjorie's brothers and sisters, they did real well in Seattle because they were citizens and they could be able... They were a draftman with the city of Seattle. Well, see, so she could have, but she didn't. A girl a little bit different. In 22 or 23. A girl a little different. Had you thought of...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Had you?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Yes. Yeah. They do. If they don't find anybody over here that they like to marry, they would go back to marry. Especially the students that have a scholarship, they would be... You know, they must go back, you see, and find the work over there. And the whole family.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: If you went back and got married, then would you be able to bring your wife back over here?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: No, you didn't. You stay there. That is rough. Yeah, that is rough. Oh, every so often they change... Well, of course, like I say, since 1943, they have changed. I mean, we can become citizens. That's when my ninth was become a citizen. Although he's been here ever since 1907 or whatever, and I've been here ever since 1920. We were not able to until then.

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Well, I was going to ask you about a little bit about the restaurant and running it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And I was wondering, was the Chinese food very popular at that time?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: No. It was not popular. Chinese food did not become popular until after the Second World War. Until after our war, it came back from overseas, especially from Korea and from other countries, you know. When they come back here, they do like the Chinese food more.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So you didn't serve Chinese food then?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Very little.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: It was American food?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Mostly American food, yes. And you see, so in order to compete with the other restaurants, we have to either serve better prepared meals in a little bit bigger portion or cost a little bit less. And the only way we survived was because we worked a little bit harder, a little bit longer hours, quite a bit longer hours, and in order to survive with the rest of the restaurants.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you get bigger portions for less money? Or what did you do? Is that what you did?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: You do that part of it. You can still always go into a Chinese restaurant and get a better proportion. Well, I would say not all the cooks, you know, that is the hard help, but most of the smaller restaurants are the owners do their own cooking and servicing. And before you have that, you have better quality food and better seasoned food, you know. Well, naturally, you would be more interested in your own living, while our hard help doesn't have to be interested, you see. They're interested in putting in so many hours for their wages. Whether you have business not like... You have to... You have to take care of that part of it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How many hours did you work on this day, say?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: In the old days, we used to work 16 hours. 14 to 16 hours. Well, 13, 14 hours, 16 hours on Saturday, they say, sometimes, because by the time you get the drunks out of there, then we have to clean up, then you know, then watch the dishes and do... And make a little preparation. Saturday, I would interrupt the old days. Well, 12 hours is our normal hours. So, if the other restaurant owners only work 8 hours, you see, well, we got the 4 hours in there, and so we should make a living just as good as they do, and we do have to. You do have to work harder, yes. And so, for 14 hours, 16 hours, it's not rare.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it hard for you to take, to adjust to, to work so much?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: No, because I've been used to it. I've been used to it ever since I was 16 years old.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When you were helping them?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: I was helping my father and mother. You get used to it like anything else, but you get used to it. After all, of course, 16 hours, those days, you weren't busy like you do in the summer in Russia, you know, they're not that way. You always have, in the afternoon, quiet time. You see, it's usually the hour of the afternoon, you get quiet time, so you can sit down and have some coffee, raise your legs or anything you want to, and then you have to get a lunch. See, that way. See, you didn't, of course, you could open up and then, like assembly line, you worked for 16 hours, you couldn't do it. But we spent 16, 14, 15, 16 hours in the restaurant.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Six days a week?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Seven days a week. You don't have any day off that, I'll go there. No, you're quite a lot of people. Well, you know, even our children, as close as, they know us and as close as we are to them, you know, they still couldn't believe some of the hard times that we had when we first started. You know, I mean, our children, they know how, well, of course, later, well, in the, in the later years, we didn't have to work that long, you know, we take it a little bit easier, you see. Well, in the first place, you have to, as you get older, past 35, you have to take it a little bit easier. You cannot work 16, you cannot be in the place 16 hours without a little bit of rest. So, my takes a split shift, you know, he would go home and rest for about half an hour to 45 minutes. Well, he could really have quiet and maybe at least rest the legs, you know, muscles, you know, and leg. When you first do it, you know, your leg muscles ache, that is, in the restaurant business. In the waitress job, especially where you run back and forth like that. And, of course, in the cooks job, you have to stand in one spot. And we have cement kitchen, and rubber mats on top. But it's still hard on, if you just stand any place, even on the run for, you know, 14, 15 hours a day, it's still hard.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you have your doubts when you first, when you were first in the business, your doubts about being able to make a go of the restaurant?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: We didn't have any doubts at all. Because my father had a restaurant when he was in the wild water. And he learned quite a few things, you know, from it. We used to make our own tamales and our own chilies and anybody who knows how to make those know it's a tedious work job. And Chinese food is a tedious job because everything is cut fine. You know, celery, onion, and mushrooms and all, and bamboo shoot, and water chestnut, they're all cut fine. And that takes time. And in the old days, we could not afford to have slicers, you know, and like mushrooms and stuff. You can't do it anyway with a slice.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you serve any Chinese food on the menu? Do people want this?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Oh, yes. We had Chinese food and American food both. Even in the early days. Very little Chinese food because right along, oh, from years back, they always had been noodles and fried rice and chop soy, you see. And so those that had always been favorites, but now they have so many different kinds. They have a press dutch and the, you know, and the soy chicken and the almond chicken and all of that, you see, and the shrimp and so on and so forth.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were those real Chinese style cooking at that time that you did?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Well, they say chop soy is not a Chinese dish. It is a Chinese dish. Only maybe we put in a few things like bamboo shoot. There are many different types of bamboo shoot, I mean in different sauces. There is a bamboo shoot that is very pungent. You could smell it all over the restaurant. There is a Chinese bamboo shoot that is not so pungent, you know, I mean that. And while the Chinese people like to put a little or something like that in it because they are accustomed to eating it. And so they know that the American people are not used to eating that. So they don't put that in there, but they know what onion is. They know what celery is and they know what bean sprouts are. Well, bean sprouts, they have to be brought to the attention of the American people. That's another new thing. And all these variations in squid and abalone and all this stuff, you know, well, in the old days probably some American people, abalone, oh, oh, you know, like that, see. Well, Chinese people have that for years. And so they know what abalone is. Well, a lot of, many of the younger people who don't know what squid is, who wants to eat a meal with squids, you know, oh, they think about the claws, you know, and the tentacles. Well, they don't know what delicacy it is. You have to know those things. So, chop story is a version, it is a Chinese dish, except that we leave out some of the things and then we add some of the things at the end. Because the American people are used to, see, and so it's a, and the noodles, you have spaghetti and you have macaroni, well, noodles is a Chinese, it's a smaller cut of, you know, spaghetti and things. But you knew that you wouldn't have any trouble or that you'd be able to make a decent living there. We knew that if we work hard, we can make a decent living. We make a living, not a decent living, but a warm -up garden with a new people. Well, during the depression year, we were, we just, well, in fact, we did, like I say, we owe money to, you know, I'm sure other, other rest do too. Because if we do it, I know other people who do it just as well, we owe some money to the cooks and we owe some money to the other waitresses. No, I mean, for three months and wait until the, the very months come, you know, during the summertime, it's a better month and during the wintertime when the snow's on the ground, less people go out to eat, you know, and because they have to keep their, keep their home warm anyway, so they might just want to cook a little food at home. And so they don't go out to eat well. So during the wintertime, maybe we have to tell the people that we have to owe them the money and they have to wait until summertime until we could make it before we pay them back wages, you see. But we have no trouble with our workers trusting that we will pay them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you employ many people then to help you when you first started?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Not too many. We always have employees, yes.

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: And I always try to have university students who need a job. Oh, we used to have some of the best boys that would help us, you know, scrub the floor, wash windows and help with the dishes and peel potatoes and things. We don't have potato paste and all that stuff. We use real potatoes, you know, I mean, they have to do all that and they would grab to have a job and we still have contact with some of those workers that we used to have years ago. Just the other day, last year we saw some down there at June, at my ranch book. And he's a pharmacist now and we remember him because he's one of the fellas of that, you know, we liked Roy much.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you try to employ Chinese people when you could who needed work?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: No, no. Not too many Chinese people would like to come to a small town like this. The Chinese men don't like to come to a town where there's nothing socially for them. In a bigger town like Seattle, San Francisco, Portland, you know, they have other members, you know, that they can go to the clubs to pay cards or whatever, you know, when they're in Wudzstan. But here in Moscow, they don't have that, you see, so not too many and not too many single men would come over. And family and people, we couldn't afford to pay enough for a man to, for a family at that time. But we usually try to, of course, you know, with us having Chinese food and American food, we always try to, you know, we do have to have somebody who knows how to cook Chinese food there all the time, too. But we have American cooks who have been with us many, many years.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Who cook Chinese food, too?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: No, no, just American food. I see. And now, like noodles and things, well, we have the noodles ready, we have the pork already for him to slice, you know, and all he has to do is very little. He can do that, see, you know. Final stages. Yeah, the final stages. He can do that. Just to dish it up, like you say.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did your kids help in the business when they were growing up?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Yes, they all helped.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When did they start helping?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Oh, they start as soon as they can do anything. Like about six years old, they can peel onions and strip the celery and peel old potatoes. And in the summertime, they can peel fruit because in the old days, we used to do a lot of us doing the fast food when the food was cheaper. And they can. And yes, as soon as they can do any work, they're there to help. And of course, the younger ones help with less hours, you know, and less work and whatever they can do. That's why the girls, they work out in the dining room and the boys work in the kitchen. But the boys has worked in the dining room, too, and the girls has worked in the kitchen, too. They know all the ups and downs of both the kitchen and the dining room. So now, even when the girls are teachers, they still, you know, can work in the restaurants.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You know, I was thinking, too, when you raised your kids, do you feel that you raised them in more in the old country, traditional way or in an American way?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: More modern, more modern than traditional because I have been over here since I was 10 years old and had contacts with American ways. And most of my friends in town here or around me are American people. So I see what they're doing. And then our children go play with other children, too, you know, and they have contact with more American people than Chinese people. So I think they're raised more in the American tradition than the Chinese. But it sounds like still the working and the helping in the family is something that a lot of the youngsters don't do. Yes. Or don't do as much as your kids, probably. Yes. I agree with you. But then, you know, some of the older people, like in my age, you know, their children work pretty hard, too, like farm people. Well, just like Russian people, they're just working the farm. At first they go pick eggs up, you know, when they're four or five years old and then gradually work up to doing more and more and more things.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about the values of the kids, like, you know, honesty and those things that are really important things? Did you feel that that was the parents' place to teach the kids?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: I think so. I think it's a parent's place to teach the children and not leave it all to the teachers because the teachers do not have time to teach them the basics in school. They have 27 children each class. You only have one child at a time, like I say, and we teach them a little bit more. And I do believe that women should stay home if they possibly can. And I don't like to see women outside of the home because I think that is one of the faults that we have in the United States today. Why the children are as they are the teenagers who do things because they have too much time to waste because when they go home, there's no mother there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: There's nobody to answer to, so why should they go home?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Sometimes the children are home and even the parents are not home. But you yourself had to work when your kids were going home. Well, but we don't expect our children to work as hard as we do. But I mean, you had to work and you just don't expect your kids to, yeah. Well, I expect them to work, but actually, we give them more time. I think that's one thing that my and I feel that we should have a little bit more time for our studies than we did at the time. And maybe just a little bit more time to have a little bit more social contact with other people too. Well, I don't regret it, but because, well, we did work pretty hard, you know. But it did not injure our health in any way, except that we have to give up, you have to give up something in order to get something else.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But say your daughters who are teaching now, they're not going to be staying at home that much when they teach, do they?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: I mean, they have to be out there in a classroom and all that. Yes, but the only daughter that has a home now is my oldest daughter. And every chance she gets when she tries to stay home. And of course, since she has three children now, I've been urging her to not teach anymore and stay home more. But she says she feels that teaching, she enjoys teaching so much that she has to give up some of her enjoyment with the children. But she does have good health with her children. She has good babysitters for them when they're young. And then they go to nursery school and then they go to kindergarten. And then now one is in kindergarten, one's in nursery school, and one's with the babysitter. So with all that, and then my son -in -law has his own business, so part of the chores of raising the children falls on his hand too, and he has some time to do that. Of course, when she goes so early in the morning and don't come back until the evening, she teaches that very... So all day long, there's no contact with her at all, except Saturday and Sunday.

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: But she feels that she enjoys teaching though, she won't give up teaching. I had read that in the early days, when the Chinese women that were over here, in the first day, very seldom went out in public at all.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So what I read, is that true?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Oh, that's in the very old days. Those are the real old days. That was the custom. Gradually, they're becoming more modernized. When they cut off their hair, you know, when they're allowed to have bobbed hair, well then things begin to change.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When do you think the discrimination started to end, or to really change, so you could see that it wasn't as bad? After the Second World War, what did you say?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: The one that really did it, is the colored people. That really started pushing it. The American public were rather accepted to the Chinese more than they were the accepted to the colored people. I mean, the middle... So that would be back, say, in the 50s, when they started the civil rights... After the Second World War.

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: That's where it started.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did it change?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: It really changed for the better, really. It is. I don't, in a way, the colored people did have the wrong other area. Also, the Indians, the Indians too, have the bad end of it. They still have some of the Indians, really, that are... We've got the bad end, but we're very patient people. We're going to adapt ourselves either way. We're going to adapt ourselves. We can go into... The Chinese people go into Africa to make a living just as well as they're going to go out here and then in the Lapuhe down here. The Chinese can go down to the Indian village and make a living. We're a white man. Off of that way. There's a lot of things. We're a lot more flexible in a lot of ways that way. Well, you know, looking at the very early days when things were really rough, and even maybe up to when you were a little kid, I would think that a lot of people would have felt a lot of resentment. And when they'd come back to China, let's say, they must have had some pretty awful stories about what the United States... What kind of place it was for Chinese people. The Chinese people have it within the United States. It's a good country to make a living because you're...

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Your money... In the old days, the money is exchanged. The American money exchanged. The Chinese money was either 5 or 6 to 1. So if you save a dollar here, you got $6 over there. Wherever it was $6, it only cost you $2 to live over there. You save 4, so you have that exchange. But if the guy comes over here and spends a dollar, like a lot of them do, you're doing that and you can live in China just as well as you can live here. Well, it sounds like a good place to make a living, but not a great place to live. Well, it's the old times. They had a hard time. The labor and the people get after the Chinese people. In the books, they would... The least little injustice they felt when they go and shoot them. And the judges, they also agree with them. You know, if I had a mortgage, I'd deal like that. You don't make an issue with anything. I saw, too, in that reading that I was doing how much the American... the labor movement was against the Chinese. The labor movement went overhead.

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Well, they still are. No, they're not too bad. No, no, not against the Chinese. But still, the labor movement, some of the movement that they're doing is not very good. Oh, yeah, they're bound to be that way. They're bound to be that way. It would be a long, long time before all men are created equal. A long, long time.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You know, talking about what it was like in Walla Walla, one thing I didn't ask you about before, I was thinking about all those men there, and virtually no hardly any women at all, was there some prostitutes in Walla Walla?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Oh, yeah, there are prostitutes in Walla Walla. At that time, they had a whole street to parter to. One block was full of saloon and a prostitute...

speaker: Marie Lee Lew
words: Wait a minute, Chinese prostitutes?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: No, no, no, no. They're not... I think there was a lot of women there. Maybe there was one, maybe there was a lot of coming in. But the prostitute just snuggled up. Right back in the Chinatown, there used to be two blockings full of saloons and prostitutes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh, would they take Chinese money?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Oh, yeah, sure they'd take Chinese money. In fact, I would say they'd prefer it for the Chinese money because... Our gentlemen, more gentlemen, they don't have to drop the gun and try to slug a girl or something like that. I bagged it that way, that would have happened. You mentioned really poor families in China that men could even wind up selling his daughter. Yeah, they do. They do sell their daughter. That went on... Oh, yes. Well, some of them sell their daughter just for servants. That's how even an average family can buy a servant. They raise them, you know, and they treat them pretty nice, you know, I mean, but she had no wages to save for herself. When she get to be married to go over age, well, then they marry her. And just like, of course, they don't get the best of things, you know, but then they are treated pretty nice. They buy these. Of course, that depends on the person. Yeah, yeah. You're like the kind of people, you know, the slaves down there. If you get one, the Lord, the person, the owner of you is good, his history is good. The same thing as the little girl there. We have, as far as I can tell, most of the average Chinese people in China, a lot of them got a lot of money. They do have a little girl, raise your little girl. Then she does cleaning up and helping.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They raise her in the family?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Yes, they do. They sleep with the family all the same time. But she works in the house. Yeah, she works in the house. A lot of them go to school, too. Yeah, some go to school. Just like the old children, except they don't adopt the name, you know. They don't get any of the money out of the... She still might have parents there. Yeah, yeah. You see, you buy that child from her parents. From her parents. She still keeps her own name. I imagine if the master wants to give the name, which is very unusual, they don't usually give them their name, you know. But they raise them all... As soon as they're ready to be able to work, maybe about 10 -year -old, 8 or 9, 10 -year -old, you know. And then maybe if they like a girl, a nice family, and the family maybe is poor, you know, and they want to help them out. Maybe this family has about 6 or 8 children, you know. But they never sell their boys, no matter how hard up they are. They don't sell their boys. They sell the girls. And they just sell them and have nothing else to do with them. The other family can do whatever they want to do with a girl. And they sell some of them, especially the beautiful girls, they sell them into prostitution too. Well, these girls, they were sold to a family.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would they have a chance to make a marriage?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
