words: Oh, yeah. They would have a chance to make a good marriage. As good as the master's family can get a whole lot of them for. The master would like to, if a girl had been very nice to them, the master would try to get the best. But of course, she couldn't get a well -to -do son, you know, because there are many, well, the master's own daughters would be marrying into a better home and better, more wealth and more, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think that continued, say, like in the big Chinatowns, like in San Francisco?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: No.

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: It's just everybody is equal. Well, you know what commonness is. Everybody's equal. You have to work. If you're able to work, you have to work for your meals.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did your mother say that she lived pretty well in China because of the money that your father was making over here, that he could send back some? Did that mean she could live pretty good?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Well, she's average. She lived pretty good, yeah. She lived, you know, I would say she lived about 67%, you know, above the 50 % mark. Because of the trouble with China, they used to be only about eight or ten percent rich, and then all the 90 % were practically all of them, more poor, and they're in the middle. There's no middle in there. So, that meant, though, that your father, he could definitely do a lot better here than he could do there. Oh, yeah, that's gliding for her. That's why he's here. That's why he had to give up his wife and, you know, and the companionship of his wife in China, and to come over here and make a living.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did he get to go back?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: He's your family. You told me that he went back once to live for a while, and then came back here again.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did he get to go back very often to see her?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: No. He only lived twice, that's all. Well, he can go back as often as he want to if he has the money to do it, and if he's willing to spend the money. If his father had more money, you know, he would have gone oftener. But because it costs for the fair, and then while he's over there, he would be losing his wages over here, you see, and besides spending over there for his part, you know. And he being over here from the United States going back there, he'll have to help some of the poor one, maybe give them 50 cents or half a dollar, because he was able to do a little bit better than his neighbor. So he had to help out his neighbor, or give him a dollar or so, you see. So that costs money, too. And you know, when anybody coming back from overseas, well, all you don't know how many relatives you have, really, because everybody's related, maybe some are closer, some are fathers, but they're all your relatives.

speaker: Marie Lee Lew
words: Hello? No, he isn't? No? Is he supposed to work? Have you called his house?

speaker: Marie Lee Lew
words: Well, I would... Fixed, as far as, you know, how well off they were when they went back, as compared to the other people there. Would they do... Oh, they were better than... well, they were better than the average Chinese people over there, yes, but they were just average over here. They didn't have too much, but with money exchanged, what money they did have was exchanged into Chinese money. They did pretty well. They were able to build some apartment houses in Nanking, which was a very thriving city at that time, in 1933. And they did very well until the Japanese come in. And then everything's gone now. They have nothing whatsoever left because the communists took over after that. Oh, they went back... after the Japanese war, they went back for a short time, and they were okay until 1949, until communists come in. And then whatever rent they have have to be given away to the government, and they were able to live in their own home for nothing, and just enough from their rent to survive. And whatever jewelry they have ever had, it's been taken over.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think that they were opposed to communists?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: No, no. Everybody's taken off. Everybody had to give up that. But I mean, do you think they...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: your parents accepted communism there?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Or did they... You have to accept it until you don't have to. They have to accept it, as long as they're there. And they did. Until my mother came out in 1960, and my dad came out in 1962. So now they're in Honolulu.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think that they were very glad to get out of China?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Oh, yes, they were very glad. In fact, they were so glad that they talked it over. Whoever gets out will stay out and not go back, even if they have to be separated. So, in 1960, there were four children who were over here, one in Sydney, Australia. Three children over here in the United States, and one over there in Australia. We all worked for getting out of communist China. And we applied for both of them to come out. And also my mother -in -law, too, my husband's mother, to come out. There were three. They were all old in the 70s and 90s. And only my mother was allowed out in 1960 to see us in Hong Kong just for a week then. So she stayed out. And she waited in Hong Kong until 1962. Finally, when the people figured that my mother's never coming back anyway, they said, well, my father's getting older now. Maybe they'll just let him come out, too. So they allowed him out in 1962. And so because we, us three children are over here, and my folks had been over here for years. My father had been over here since 1910. Since 1910, and they had never done anything wrong, had always been good citizens, as well as good citizens. They were allowed to come back over here. Of course, there was a lot of red tapes, and we had to have many affidavits. And many people helped out in order for us to get them over here.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Why were they really wanting to get out? What was their objection to the amount?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: They were not free. That's reason enough. They were not free to do anything. And everything you have, anything you have, anything you ever have will have to be given to the government and share with everybody else. Where you worked your lifetime, you know, and have been thrifty and worked hard in your younger years, and then you get a little bit saved up in your old age, in your seventies, and then have everything taken away from you except for just existence, you know. That isn't very free, you know. All your jewelries gone, well, whatever. They didn't have too much, but whatever they had, it was all gone. My mother came out with fifty cents. My father was out anything. She just had enough, my mother had just enough money to come to see me in a rickshaw. Fifty cents. The way that you and Mi described the conditions in China, though, before, like in the old days, it sounds like you could hardly call the people free at that time either, really, if most everyone was poor. At that time, if you are willing to work hard, you can make a living, but not a good living. You can make a living. But I would say the majority have a sub -living condition, yes, over fifty percent, I would say, even seventy percent. And the rest, well, sixty -five or so, and maybe twenty percent in the medium class, where you can have a business your own or work in the higher wages bracket, you know, in the lower bracket, you just barely make enough. Not enough to send your children to school, maybe, but the poor ones, many starve to death. But you have a choice. You can talk as you want to, you can go to church if you want to, you can go visit your family if you want to, if you have the money to go. You are free to do as you want, but when communists come in, you are not free to go anyplace. You have race name, and your relative cannot take you in because they cannot afford to feed you on their own race ration. And when you leave your home, you have to tell the authorities where you're going, how many days you're going to be there, and when you're going to come back. And you cannot go to church, they don't have a church. They don't allow you to have a church. They don't allow you to have any meeting of more than three people. You can even have a meeting in your own home with your own relatives. That's nothing. But for everybody, 100 % of the people, they have enough to eat. They don't have to die of starvation. Nobody has to die of starvation. The poor and the sick, they are given food. The able, they have to work for the food, and so much clothing. They get so many yards of goods. You make so many, maybe one or two outfits a year, whatever. And very dull colors. You can, they only wear, I think, four colors. Black, white, gray, and brown, I think. If we, as tourists, go in there, we can go in if they let us in, and we're allowed to bring so much cloth in for our relatives. And one bicycle, one new bicycle, and one new wristwatch, and one old wristwatch, and give it to your relatives. And then that's all checked, because when your relatives wear our new wristwatch, authorities want to know where they come from. Because if your relatives didn't give it to you, you must have stole it from somebody. And if you stole anything, you're punished. But everybody's equal, and nobody, there's nothing to steal. And if you lost something, you forget something, you drop something, they pick it up and give it back to you. Or put it at the loss and file so that you can go back there and get it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, do you figure then that for most people that think it's good at what's happened?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: You think it's maybe harder on your parents because of the life they had. They had been in America and other places, and they had a chance to take more of this. That's right. More luxury. And for the rich people that have been in China, they are pretty badly off. Of all the real rich and the real wealthy and real important figures, they are killed. Killed, yes. The head man of everything is killed. The most intelligent people, the most well educated. Unless you hurry up and tell them that you believe in the very... But many, many millions have been dead killed. Even my mother -in -law, she didn't do anything wrong, but she sold a piece of probably ten years before that, you know. They want to know what the money is. They want that money. She said, well, I've been living for ten years.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How could I have any money now?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: So they knocked her out and she lost her front teeth in her 80s. Was it someone, a Red Army... The authorities. I wanted to ask you too about the contact that you've kept with the Chinese friends around here. I was wondering about that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you feel that through the years you've kept a fairly close contact with the other Chinese people in the area?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Oh yes. With the friends that we have, you know, and they're close to us here. We go visiting all the restaurant people, you know, in Lewiston, Pullman, and Colfax, Othello, and Moses Lake. Of course, we do more than other people do, you know, because we're retired now and we can go and come as we please. But every so often if somebody gets married or, I mean, their children get married, you know, we all gather someplace. Just not very long. Last month we were all in Spokane. Tommy's second boy got married. We were all invited for a Chinese dinner there. We were invited to their wedding down in Los Angeles too, but it's too far. So, Maya and I didn't go, but some of the people went. But for around this part of the country, the friends couldn't go to Los Angeles. And they have a feast down there too after the wedding reception, and then they have a dinner, a nice dinner for the couple, you know. And the friends all, you know, down there invited down there. And then so he feel that the friends up here, you know, didn't get a chance. And he invited us over here, see. And then last year we were invited to Othello when their oldest daughter was married. And so, you know, all our friends, when their daughters or sons married, you know, if there's a big wedding and a big dinner, all the people around here are invited. The thing is, most of these around Moscow are all Ings, and we're the only lose, but we were very glad that we were invited to all the Ings affairs anyway, you know. And I'll lose a small family, and so we don't have too many. And most of our relatives now, I don't have any more relatives in Spokane. We had gone already. My cousins at Washington Noodle Cafe in Spokane, they all died. Washington Noodle Cafe. That was there for years. And now their children, they went to Washington State and some went to Seattle, University of Seattle, and then they all scattered. Some went to New York to do their thing, and some went to San Francisco, some went to Los Angeles, you know, all different places now. They are my cousins then, you see. My uncles in there are dead. Of course, my father outlived them all, you see. They died in their 60s, and my father is 86 now. My mother's 87. So he is one of the few that outlived most of our relatives. And then my relatives are over in Seattle on the coast. So, whatever we go, we go to the coast, we go to Seattle and stop there, and we stop at Pasco. We have friends there at Pasco and Walla Walla, where am I from, you know. And we go there. We still have a very good friend down there, just one left now. And now his son and children are running the farm down there now. Because he's still there, but he just looks around, you know, not doing very little work. Whatever he can do, he'll do.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Are the Ings that are around here, your friends, have they been here for many years?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Yes. The ones in Lewiston have been here for a long time. These people, you know, Tommy Eng, been over there, I know, since 1920. I think he went to high school in the 24, 25, somewhere around there. My was working for his father, Tommy Eng's father, when he was a freshman at Washington State in 25.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: In Pullman?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: In Pullman, at the Oriental. And then Freddie, who was with him there, went to Colfax for a while, and now he's moved over there to Othello. So we go in contact with them quite a bit. Whenever we go to Seattle, we always stop there, you see.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where does Tommy Ing live now?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Tommy Ing is still in Pullman. He's still in Pullman, yes. Now he has three sons, two of his sons, over there in Pullman with him. One son wanted to stay down there in Los Angeles. They all graduated from universities. I think Kenny, the oldest one, did too, but I'm not sure. But he had been with his father a long time, and now the second boy is with his father now.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Are these the people, those that you consider your closest friends, the Ings and the others?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Well, they are the friends, yes, very close. And of course we're very close to Poi and Susie down here at the New Hong Kong. They bought our place, of course, you know. And practically every day we run down there, you know. We'd have coffee, and when they're busy we beat it, you know. And then when they're not busy we stop and gab. Sometimes she makes something extra special, and then we get, she sends one of the kids up with it, you know. Sometimes when I make a good banana -mole bread or something, I bring one down to her, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How long have they been here since then? Them?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Yes. They've been around, they're from, they're from Walla Walla originally, and then over in the Colfax they work for Pete Ng. That's the cousin of Tommy Ng in the Oriental. And then he is the uncle of Yik, over here at the Chinese village. Poi is the uncle there, see. Poi's the one that owns the Hong Kong. The New Hong Kong, yeah, Poi and Susie. And he is the uncle of the man at the... Yik's, at the Chinese village. See, they are, the other way they're related. And then Tommy and Poi and the people in the, down loose, and some of them are from the same village in Canton. See, Ng's.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Which village is that?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: I don't know which village. I was just thinking, it wasn't Kliyep, the word. Because you mentioned Kliyep.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Kliyep?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Yeah, before that. No, Kliyep, Samyep. Those are the three counties and four counties. They're the counties. But, you know, I've forgotten so much already.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That's the other thing I was going to ask you, again, why was it that everybody came from Canton? Why is that the place that people came from?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: They're the people that are more business people. Some of the other people are more farming people. So, farming and fishermen, they stay home. See, the business people, the commercial people, they go out. You always find the business people are the ones that go out to look for their fortune, you know. They're the ones that come over here to America, you know. Of course, they are the church people who are being kicked out for political reasons or for church reasons. But they also, probably, they're the more aggressive people who want to get out and make a better living than they've had, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Hey, did you find him?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: You didn't. I better find Mortz. I should go there. Oh, okay, yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But you say religious, religious reasons to people left from Canton?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: No, no, not religious, no. Because they are commercial people and business people. They are more inclined to travel.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Isn't that right, Mike?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: The Cantonese says, go out more.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Why so many people came from Canton more than any place else?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: I thought I did too...

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Yeah, I thought you did too, but I'm not sure that I got what I... Yeah, I'm talking about that because during the early days, when the people were on railroads, you know, the railroad bill had the Chinese people of labor in the world. They asked the Manchu government. The Manchu government, the Chinese, the Cantonese are always the one, the naughty one. Oh, that's it. Yeah, they, the Manchus, didn't get along. He's the black sheep of the Chinese people. So the best way to get the rhythm, the Manchus still love the Chinese, the Cantonese, but they're the naughty ones. So they send the men out.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Hello, could you tell me?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: They did not let the wife go, so they just come back.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Are you supposed to be having a little more?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Something's happening there tonight. Since the naughty one didn't do that at all.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You're sure that he's not there?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Because he's... Okay, thank you. He's not there. Yeah, so that's it because they and the Manchus, they kind of were running China at the... The dynasty. That's what I thought. Yeah, well, that's for the first Chinese immigrants over here. But then lately, I mean, after that, after the first group of laborers come over here, you know, but after that, it's still the Cantonese people come over here. And they did come over here for the radio. Because the Manchus want to keep the Northern people, they're the most peaceful people. So they keep them home. The Northern ones want to send them out and let them live with their living. What the Cantonese got out, you see. Therefore, they know the rope. So therefore, naturally, they would pull out the ribbon. They're more likely to want to go. They know the people who are going to have their children and the Manchus to come over. They know what it's all about. Or the other ones, they stay home. They can take them what they got home.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you talk about China a lot when you get together with your friends? I just was wondering, did you talk about the old country?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: No, we don't get together often enough to talk about the old country. Well, if somebody's going back, you know, and they say they're going back, and we talk a little bit. And then, especially after we made a trip to Hong Kong or someplace, you know, then we tell them what the changes is now and all that. But we don't talk about too much of what's going on there because they don't know any more than we do. So we just don't talk too much about the old times. Yeah, and we don't talk about the old times.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You don't?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Because, you see, I've been over here since I was a child, so there's no old time over there for me, except during my childhood, and those few years I was going to school. So there's nothing old time, you know. And of course, Susie down there, she's had a rough time getting away from her home and getting out into Hong Kong, and this is during the hard time, you know, when the communists come over, getting out, and it was hard for her to get around and scratch around for her brothers and sisters. She was really the one that's taking care of them. So she could tell you more about that than I can. I just was wondering if you talked about what you talked about. No, we just have enough time to talk about all what's going on now, and once in a while I asked them if they always, some of them, so we'll take the Chinese newspaper, see. Because I don't read too much, I don't read hardly any Chinese, and so I don't. And they tell me about the same thing that I get from the American newspaper, so there isn't too much difference, there wasn't too much news that I could get from them, through the newspaper that I could get from the American newspaper.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When you and Mi were married, did you have a lot of people come for that?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: We only have a small American ceremony. We were married, like I say, by Reverend Drury. We are Christians, you see, so not all the Chinese people over here are Christians. Even some of those people who are down there are majestic, they're not.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Are the Ings mostly Christian or not?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: I don't think so. I don't think they are, maybe the children go to Sunday school, but my age people were the only ones that are Christians, really. Because my father and mother were Christians from back in China.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about Mi and his father, was he a Christian?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: No, he wasn't, until... Oh yes, he was in Walla Walla, because there were some church ladies there, the Chinese boys, to go to a church in Sunday school, so he started when he was in Sunday school. That's where he learned most of his good habits, and go along with whatever the church has to offer. Him not having a home, actually, just his father having one room, one bedroom for him and my, and then his father would, and the rest, all the time, he sent him a mine to go to school. The only thing he had socially would be the church, connected with the church, and the very few, I think he told me two or three boys that he knows that their folks invite him to their home. When they have a birthday or something, he has cake and ice cream with them, but just a few. He keeps up with them, you know, and he went to high school, and so, and in fact, just about three years ago, he had his 50th high school reunion.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think that made it any easier for the family, for your family to get used to American ways, because of that being Christian?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: It might be, because, you know, there's one more string leading to, besides the school, you get the church, you know, and then we, out in business, too, you see, that has a lot to do with, well, we're among the American people, you see, where a lot of the people, Chinese people, that's in San Francisco, they're right in Chinatown. They cater to the Chinese people, you know, like a grocery store, or their paintings, and stuff like that, it's mostly with the Chinese people. Then they have a lot less to do, contact, besides, for years, we were the only Chinese family here in Moscow, the only one. And I was the only high, Maryland high school, the only Chinese in high school, well, the only family, so I had to be the only Chinese, you know, in high school. And my sisters were in grade school, and then I go on to college, the only one there. And I don't think there was any Filipino girls there, either, I don't remember any Filipino girls. Filipino teacher, you know, for the year, the ball. Oh, uh -huh. Yeah, Filipino teacher, you speak. But now, we have six or seven or eight Chinese professors up here at the university.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That was the only fellow Filipino teacher, right?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Seven or eight, the university. Oh, yeah, at the university, yeah. And there was quite a few at Washington State. Yeah, there were quite a few. So, and...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What do they mostly teach now, you know, the English?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: English. One is ahead of the English department. English, and we've been to Fourscreme, or... This is a Chinese association. We're going to have a potluck dinner this coming Saturday. Oh.

speaker: Marie Lee Lew
words: You want to come along?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I'd like to.

speaker: Marie Lee Lew
words: Well, there'll be Chinese food there, and I think it'll be okay, don't you?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Ask me, I have a question for you. I'm afraid of my... Sure, it's okay, I'm sure. I would ask the Irish members and friends. I can ask Laura if...

speaker: Marie Lee Lew
words: Your wife?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Yeah, if she'd... Okay, okay. Yeah, I see. You say five -thru...
